description:
You are working as a junior backup-and-restore engineer who has just received a freshly
    generated backup package together with its checksum file.  
    Your two goals are:

    1. Verify the integrity of the backup data.  
    2. Draft (but **do not apply**) a minimal iptables rules file that
       will later be used to protect the backup server.

    The system has already been prepared with the following directory and files:

        /home/user/backup/
            ├── weekly_backup.dat
            └── weekly_backup.sha256

    -------------- PART 1 – DATA-INTEGRITY CHECK --------------
    • Use exactly one standard Linux tool of your choice
      (e.g., `sha256sum -c`) to verify that the checksum in
      /home/user/backup/weekly_backup.sha256 matches the
      content of /home/user/backup/weekly_backup.dat.

    • Capture the plain, unmodified **stdout** that your verification
      command prints and save it into
      /home/user/backup/integrity_check.log.

      Required final content of the log file (including the newline):

          weekly_backup.dat: OK

      The automated tests will look for the exact string above
      with a single trailing newline character.

    -------------- PART 2 – FIREWALL DRAFT --------------
    • Create a new text file
      /home/user/backup/firewall_rules.backup.conf  
      with **exactly two lines** (each terminated by a Unix newline):

          -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 873 -j ACCEPT
          -P INPUT DROP

      Line 1 allows rsync traffic on TCP port 873 from any source.  
      Line 2 makes every other inbound packet fall through to a DROP
      policy.  Do **not** add blank lines, comments or spaces that are
      not shown above.

    -------------- CONSOLE SUMMARY LINE --------------
    After you finish both steps, print to **stdout** a single, final,
    human-readable summary line **and nothing else**.  
    The automated grader parses this line to confirm that you performed
    the requested work:

        CHECKSUM_VALID=true; FIREWALL_FILE=/home/user/backup/firewall_rules.backup.conf

    • Use the exact casing, spacing and punctuation shown.
    • Do not print anything before or after this line.
    • The value of CHECKSUM_VALID should be the literal word “true”
      if the checksum matched, otherwise “false”.

    You should be able to accomplish the entire task using only
    2–3 shell commands.  Root privileges are **not** required and must
    not be used.
truth:
Initial file system state (already present when the task starts):

    1. Directory  
       Path: /home/user/backup  
       Permissions: 0755 (user-writable)

    2. File  
       Path: /home/user/backup/weekly_backup.dat  
       Permissions: 0644  
       Contents (single byte, no newline):  
           a

    3. File  
       Path: /home/user/backup/weekly_backup.sha256  
       Permissions: 0644  
       Exact contents (two spaces between hash and filename, trailing newline):  
           ca978112ca1bbdcafac231b39a23dc4da786eff8147c4e72b9807785afee48bb  weekly_backup.dat\n

       (The hexadecimal string above is the SHA-256 digest of the single
        byte “a”; it matches the file shipped in #2.)

    4. Files that **must** be created by the agent:

       a. /home/user/backup/integrity_check.log  
          Expected exact contents (with trailing newline):  
              weekly_backup.dat: OK\n

       b. /home/user/backup/firewall_rules.backup.conf  
          Expected exact contents:  
              -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 873 -j ACCEPT\n
              -P INPUT DROP\n

    5. Console output the grader will capture from the agent:  
       Exactly one line (no extra whitespace or newlines):

           CHECKSUM_VALID=true; FIREWALL_FILE=/home/user/backup/firewall_rules.backup.conf
name: task_000000_0cb07b39
